title: Windsurf vs Cursor
shortAnswer: Cursor is the more feature-complete and popular AI coding IDE in 2026, with a deeper editor toolset, faster tab autocomplete, and the largest user community, making it the safe default for most developers. Windsurf (formerly Codeium) competes hard on its Cascade agent, a clean onboarding experience, and a lower Pro price of roughly $15/month versus Cursor's $20/month. If you want raw IDE breadth and momentum, pick Cursor; if you want an agent-first flow at a lower cost, Windsurf is the stronger value. Both are VS Code forks, so switching between them is low-friction. Your existing extensions, themes, and keybindings carry over to either tool.
verdict: For the price-sensitive solo developer, Windsurf wins: its Pro tier costs about $15/month versus Cursor's $20, and its free tier is generous enough to evaluate seriously. For the agentic-workflow user who wants the IDE to plan, edit, and run multi-file changes autonomously, the choice is closer, but Cursor's Agent mode currently edges ahead on reliability and ecosystem polish while Windsurf's Cascade offers a cleaner, more guided loop. For teams and enterprises, Cursor leads on admin controls, SSO, privacy-mode maturity, and the larger talent pool already familiar with it, though Windsurf's enterprise tier and self-hosting heritage from Codeium make it a credible alternative for security-conscious orgs. Pick Cursor for breadth, momentum, and team adoption; pick Windsurf for value and a friendlier agent-first experience.

faqs.question: Is Windsurf better than Cursor?
faqs.answer: Neither is universally better — it depends on what you value. Cursor is the more feature-complete tool in 2026, with the best tab autocomplete, a powerful Agent mode, and by far the largest community and momentum. That makes it the safer default for most developers and teams. Windsurf is the better value: it costs roughly $15/month versus Cursor's $20, has a clean agent-first interface built around its Cascade agent, and is friendlier to newcomers. If raw IDE breadth and ecosystem matter most, choose Cursor. If price and a guided agent experience matter more, Windsurf is the stronger pick. Because both are VS Code forks, you can trial each with minimal switching cost.

faqs.question: Windsurf vs Cursor pricing — which is cheaper?
faqs.answer: Windsurf is cheaper. Its Pro plan is around $15/month, while Cursor Pro is $20/month — a roughly 25% difference on the headline tier. Both offer free tiers: Windsurf's free plan includes a generous allotment with limited premium-model credits, and Cursor's free Hobby tier provides limited fast requests plus a two-week Pro trial. Beyond the base tiers, both tools meter premium model usage, so heavy agent use can add cost on either platform depending on which models you call. Exact tiers, credit limits, and add-ons change frequently in this fast-moving market, so confirm current pricing on each vendor's site before committing. For most solo developers, Windsurf's lower entry price is the deciding factor.

faqs.question: Cascade vs Composer — which agent is better?
faqs.answer: Cascade is Windsurf's agent and Composer (now generally called Agent) is Cursor's. In 2026, Cursor's Agent edges ahead on reliability for complex, multi-file autonomous changes and benefits from a larger ecosystem of tutorials and community workflows. Windsurf's Cascade counters with a cleaner, more guided loop — it surfaces its reasoning and tool calls in a way that's easier for newcomers to follow and trust. Both can read your whole codebase, run terminal commands, and make coordinated edits across files. If you want maximum autonomous power and polish, Cursor's Agent is the stronger choice today. If you prefer a transparent, step-by-step agent that's easy to supervise, Cascade is excellent and arguably more approachable.

faqs.question: Is Windsurf good for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes — Windsurf is one of the more beginner-friendly AI coding IDEs. Its onboarding is clean and guided, the layout is less cluttered than Cursor's, and the Cascade agent presents its steps transparently so new developers can learn from what it's doing rather than just accepting opaque changes. The lower price and generous free tier also make it low-risk to start with. That said, beginners benefit from community resources, and Cursor's larger ecosystem means more tutorials and troubleshooting threads exist for it. A reasonable approach is to try Windsurf first for its gentler learning curve, then evaluate Cursor if you need its broader feature set. Since both are VS Code forks, the core editor skills you build transfer between them.

faqs.question: Should I switch from Cursor to Windsurf?
faqs.answer: Switch only if you have a specific reason. The strongest case for moving from Cursor to Windsurf is cost — saving roughly $5/month on the Pro tier — or a preference for Cascade's guided agent style and cleaner interface. Self-hosting and enterprise security needs, inherited from Windsurf's Codeium roots, are another valid driver. However, if you rely on Cursor's best-in-class tab autocomplete, its mature Agent, or the depth of community tutorials, switching may feel like a downgrade in those areas. Migration cost is low because both are VS Code forks — your extensions, themes, and keybindings carry over. The practical move is to run Windsurf in parallel for a week on real work before deciding.

faqs.question: Windsurf vs Cursor for large codebases?
faqs.answer: Both handle large codebases well, but Cursor currently has the edge. Its codebase indexing is mature, and the @-symbol reference system lets you precisely point the model at specific files, functions, or docs, which keeps context tight in big repositories. Cursor's Agent is also more battle-tested on sprawling, multi-file changes. Windsurf is competitive: its Riptide retrieval indexing gives Cascade strong whole-repo awareness, and the agent can navigate and edit across many files. For very large monorepos where retrieval precision and agent reliability matter most, Cursor is the safer choice in 2026. If your repo is moderate in size and you value Cascade's guided flow, Windsurf will serve you well at a lower price.

faqs.question: Are Windsurf and Cursor both based on VS Code?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both Windsurf and Cursor are forks of Visual Studio Code, which is why the two feel familiar to anyone coming from VS Code. Your existing extensions, themes, settings, and keybindings generally carry over to either tool, and the core editing experience — file explorer, command palette, integrated terminal — is essentially the same. The differences are layered on top: each adds its own AI panels, autocomplete engine, and agent (Cascade for Windsurf, Agent for Cursor). This shared foundation is a big reason switching costs are low; you can install both, import your VS Code profile, and compare them side by side on the same project within minutes.

faqs.question: Was Windsurf formerly known as Codeium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Windsurf is the rebranded product from Codeium, the company that originally offered a popular free AI autocomplete extension for many editors. The Windsurf editor evolved from that lineage into a standalone, agent-first IDE built around Cascade. This heritage matters for two reasons: first, Codeium's autocomplete technology underpins Windsurf's strong Supercomplete feature; and second, Codeium had a track record with enterprise and self-hosting deployments, which carries into Windsurf's enterprise tier and appeals to security-conscious organizations. If you read older comparison articles that reference Codeium versus Cursor, they are effectively describing an earlier stage of what is now Windsurf versus Cursor — keep that in mind when weighing dated reviews.
